title: Turnstile Played Through Grief and Fury and Came Out the Other Side
subtitle: Grammy winners, mosh pits, a cello cameo, and an emotional tribute made this the most intense set of Weekend 1
author: Leelou Blanc
description: There is no version of Turnstile that holds back. That has been true since the Baltimore hardcore band started playing basements in 2010, and it was
image_alt: Turnstile Played Through Grief and Fury and Came Out the Other Side
body:
[spotify:track/0vbdCsYHg1by09QKOGO4PR]

There is no version of Turnstile that holds back. That has been true since the Baltimore hardcore band started playing basements in 2010, and it was violently, beautifully true at the Outdoor Theatre on Friday at 8:05 PM, when they played what might have been the most emotionally loaded set of their career.

## Three Mosh Pits Deep

[youtube:UEjGgpX5SA0]

Within the first thirty seconds of the opening track, three separate mosh pits had formed in the Outdoor Theatre crowd. Not the choreographed circle pits of festival hardcore, these were organic, chaotic, joyful expressions of a crowd that had been waiting all day for permission to lose it. Turnstile gave them that permission and then kept pushing.

The Grammy-winning "Birds" arrived early in the set and hit with the force of a band that knows exactly what that song means now. It's the track that took hardcore punk to the Grammy stage, the one that made rock critics and punk lifers argue about whether that was a good thing. Brendan Yates sang it like the argument was irrelevant, like the only thing that mattered was the room and the sound and the people in it. The song has lived in the cultural discourse for long enough that it carries a weight beyond its own runtime. At Coachella, in a crowd of thousands, it landed clean.

The set moved with the relentlessness that is Turnstile's signature live quality. Songs overlapped. The band did not stop to explain themselves or acknowledge the crowd between numbers in the way festival headliners often do, filling space with gratitude and stage banter. Yates spoke in bursts, not paragraphs. The music did the work.

## The Dev Hynes Moment

Midway through the set, Dev Hynes, Blood Orange himself, walked onstage carrying a cello. "Seein' Stars," already one of the best rock songs released in the last five years, became something else entirely with live strings cutting through the distortion. The collaboration made perfect sense in a way that shouldn't work on paper. Hynes, the genre-fluid art-pop polymath, sitting with a cello amid the wreckage of a hardcore set, and the music breathed differently. The mosh pits paused. People looked at each other. Then the chorus hit and everyone moved again.

The relationship between Hynes and Turnstile is one of those creative alliances that illuminates something about both parties. Hynes has spent his career dismantling genre categories, moving between R&B, ambient, electronic, and indie rock with a fluency that makes the categories look arbitrary. Turnstile, for their part, have always been more than their hardcore origins. The melodic intelligence in their songwriting, the way they use dynamics and space within a genre that often abhors both, is what distinguishes them from their peers. Hynes heard something in "Seein' Stars" worth amplifying with strings. He was right.

## Playing Through Pain

What most of the crowd didn't know, and what made the set resonate on a deeper frequency, was the context. Earlier that week, former guitarist Brady Ebert had been charged with second-degree attempted murder after allegedly hitting Brendan Yates's father, William Yates, with a car. The band did not address it directly. But after "Seein' Stars," a message appeared briefly on the screens: a tribute to Bill Yates, followed by a red tape heart in memory of Bo Lueders, the Harm's Way guitarist who died recently.

Yates stood still for a moment. The crowd held its breath. Then the band launched into the next song with a ferocity that felt less like performance and more like survival.

This is where the specificity of what Turnstile does becomes most legible. Hardcore punk has always been about processing things that are too large for polite expression. The genre was built for grief and anger and love that cannot find another outlet. Playing through something that personal, in front of thousands of people at one of the world's largest festivals, using the tools the music has always provided, is not a performance of emotion. It is the emotion itself.

## The Never Enough Cuts

The material from Never Enough sounded enormous in the open desert air. The album, which arrived to significant commercial and critical success, translated to the outdoor festival context without losing any of its compression. The production on the record, which emphasized a particular density and warmth unusual in hardcore, became something more cinematic at scale.

The older material, "Mystery," "Holiday," and selections from Time and Space, proved that the catalog is deeper than most rock bands achieve in twice the career. There was no padding in the set. Every song earned its position. The sequencing moved between intensity and brief moments of melodic release in the way the best hardcore sets do, using the audience's adrenaline as an instrument rather than treating it as a constant they have to maintain.

## Why Turnstile at Coachella Matters

Hardcore punk at Coachella has always been a negotiation, the genre's intensity versus the festival's curation, the pit versus the sponsored lounge. Turnstile doesn't negotiate. They showed up as exactly what they are: a band that has won Grammys, sold out arenas, and still plays like every show is in a Baltimore basement.

The presence of Turnstile on the Coachella bill makes a statement about where independent heavy music sits in 2026. They did not soften anything to be there. They did not adjust their set for an audience that might include casual festival-goers alongside committed fans. They played the same set they would play anywhere, which is the only respectable choice, and the crowd met them there.

Friday night belonged to a lot of artists. But at 8:05 PM, in the golden hour before the sun fully set, Turnstile owned the Outdoor Theatre in a way that felt permanent.

Follow Turnstile on Instagram: @turnstilehc
